Section 15: Linear Regression

library(tidyverse)
## Warning: package 'tidyverse' was built under R version 4.4.3

## Warning: package 'tibble' was built under R version 4.4.3

## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
# Set all plots in this section to have "minima" theme - looks cleaner
theme_set(theme_minimal())

Correlation

A scatterplot shows a downward trend between year and freeze duration.

monona <- read_csv("../../data/lake-monona-winters-2025.csv")
## Rows: 170 Columns: 10
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (3): winter, period50, ff_cat
## dbl  (5): year1, periods, duration, decade, ff_x
## date (2): first_freeze, last_thaw
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
ggplot(monona, aes(x = year1, y = duration)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

We can calculate correlation with the cor function.

x <- monona$year1
y <- monona$duration

cor(x, y)
## [1] -0.5482328
cor(y, x)
## [1] -0.5482328

Correlation examples

# Hide this chunk - just for the purpose of making example plots

set.seed(2023)
x1 = seq(-2,2,0.05)
y0 = rnorm(length(x1))
y1 = x1^2
x2 = x1[x1>0]
y2 = y1[x1>0]
x3 = x1[x1<0]
y3 = y1[x1<0]
x4 = x1
y4 = x4 + rnorm(length(x4),0,0.3)
x5 = x1
y5 = x5 + rnorm(length(x5),0,1)
x6 = x1[x1 > -1]
y6 = exp(3*x6)/10 + rnorm(length(x6), 0, 5)

cor_plot = function(x,y)
{
  ggplot(tibble(x,y), aes(x=x, y=y)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "lm") +
    ggtitle(str_c("r ≈ ", round(cor(x,y),2))) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(text = element_text(size = 20)) 
}

Correlation 0 corresponds to no linear relationship. This can be a horizontal line or some other shape.

cor_plot(x1, y0)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

cor_plot(x1, y1)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Correlation near 1 or -1 indicates that a line fits the data well. This does not necessarily imply a true linear relationship.

cor_plot(x4, y4)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

cor_plot(-x4, y4)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

cor_plot(x2, y2)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

A correlation of 0.7 indicates that a line fits the data somewhat well. Again, there maybe some other underlying relationship.

cor_plot(x5, y5)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

cor_plot(x6, y6)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

An example motivated by real life: what is the relationship between a baseball team’s winning percentage and the latitude of its stadium?

set.seed(20240406)
fake_latitude <- tibble(
  win_perc = runif(25, 0, 1),
  latitude = runif(25, 25, 48)
)

ggplot(fake_latitude, aes(latitude, win_perc)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F) +
  labs(title = "Winning Percentage vs. Latitude of Home Stadium",
       subtitle = "(Not real data)",
       y = "Winning Percentage",
       x = "Latitude (degrees)")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Now, consider predicting the winning percentage based on average wages.

set.seed(20240406)
fake_wages <- tibble(
  wages = rnorm(25, 5, 2),
  win_perc = wages* rnorm(25, 1, 0.5),
  win_perc_adj = (win_perc - min(win_perc))/max(win_perc - min(win_perc))
)

ggplot(fake_wages, aes(wages, win_perc_adj)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F) +
  labs(title = "Winning Percentage vs. Wages",
       subtitle = "(Not real data)",
       y = "Winning Percentage",
       x = "Wages (Millions of $")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Linear modeling

Exercise

riley <- read_table("../../data/riley.txt")
## 
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## cols(
##   age = col_double(),
##   height = col_double()
## )
riley_2_8 <- riley %>%
  filter(age >= 24 & age <= 96)

# Find estimates for beta_0 and beta_1

sy <- sd(riley_2_8$height)
sx <- sd(riley_2_8$age)
r <- cor(riley_2_8$height, riley_2_8$age)

beta1 <- r * (sy/sx)
beta1
## [1] 0.2505185
xbar <- mean(riley_2_8$age)
ybar <- mean(riley_2_8$height)

beta0 <- ybar - beta1 * xbar
beta0
## [1] 30.24933

We can fit a linear model automatically with lm.

lm(height ~ age, data = riley_2_8)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = height ~ age, data = riley_2_8)
## 
## Coefficients:
## (Intercept)          age  
##     30.2493       0.2505
# It's helpful to save the result to reference it later
height_mod <- lm(height ~ age, data = riley_2_8)

summary(height_mod)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = height ~ age, data = riley_2_8)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -0.29911 -0.19291 -0.03355  0.21982  0.46334 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 30.249329   0.186745  161.98   <2e-16 ***
## age          0.250519   0.002869   87.33   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.2429 on 14 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.9982, Adjusted R-squared:  0.998 
## F-statistic:  7627 on 1 and 14 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
ggplot(riley_2_8, aes(x = age, y = height)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "lm") +
  ylab("Height (inches)") +
  xlab("Age (months)")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

We can also do this for our Lake Monona data.

lake_mod <- lm(duration ~ year1, data = monona)

Assumptions

We evaluate the assumptions (i.e., validate the linear model fit) by studying the residuals. For ease of graphing, we can add the residuals to our data.

resid(lake_mod)
##            1            2            3            4            5            6 
##  -3.54365325  29.68032315  -2.09570044 -26.87172404  -9.64774763   8.57622877 
##            7            8            9           10           11           12 
##  12.80020518  -0.97581842  11.24815799  -2.52786561   5.69611080   8.92008720 
##           13           14           15           16           17           18 
## -10.85593640   7.36804001  19.59201641 -18.18400718  24.03996922  23.26394563 
##           19           20           21           22           23           24 
##  18.48792203   5.71189844 -26.06412516  15.15985125 -54.61617235  -1.39219594 
##           25           26           27           28           29           30 
## -23.16821954  44.05575687 -38.72026673  11.50370967   4.72768608   5.95166248 
##           31           32           33           34           35           36 
##  16.17563889  16.39961529  22.62359170 -27.15243190 -39.92845549  -0.70447909 
##           37           38           39           40           41           42 
## -17.48050268   3.74347372 -14.03254987  -8.80857347   6.41540293  10.63937934 
##           43           44           45           46           47           48 
## -11.13664426  17.08733215   0.31130855   6.53528496  -8.24073864 -26.01676223 
##           49           50           51           52           53           54 
##  29.20721417   0.43119058   4.65516698  -4.12085661  -3.89688021   8.32709620 
##           55           56           57           58           59           60 
## -13.44892740  -6.22495100   3.99902541  -4.77699819 -25.55302178   6.67095462 
##           61           62           63           64           65           66 
##   2.89493103   8.11890743   8.34288384 -24.43313976   8.79083665 -21.98518695 
##           67           68           69           70           71           72 
## -10.76121054  20.46276586  -3.31325774  -3.08928133  20.13469507  -3.64132852 
##           73           74           75           76           77           78 
##   2.58264788  -9.19337571  -0.96939931  -6.74542290 -44.52144650   2.70252991 
##           79           80           81           82           83           84 
##  -1.07349369  -5.84951728   3.37445912  19.59843552   7.82241193  -0.95361167 
##           85           86           87           88           89           90 
##  -0.72963526  24.49434114 -17.28168245  11.94229395  -0.83372964 -12.60975324 
##           91           92           93           94           95           96 
##  -2.38577683   6.83819957   7.06217598  -8.71384762  12.51012879  17.73410519 
##           97           98           99          100          101          102 
##  11.95808159  -6.81794200 -27.59396560 -16.36998919   9.85398721  -6.92203638 
##          103          104          105          106          107          108 
##  10.30194002  26.52591643   2.74989283  -0.02613076  15.19784564  14.42182205 
##          109          110          111          112          113          114 
##  -5.35420155  16.86977485 -31.90624874  -8.68227234  -9.45829593   4.76568047 
##          115          116          117          118          119          120 
##  -4.01034312  -2.78636672  -0.56239031  -5.33841391  -1.11443750   0.10953890 
##          121          122          123          124          125          126 
##  -4.66648469   8.55749171  26.78146812  23.00544452  10.22942092  -7.54660267 
##          127          128          129          130          131          132 
##   6.67737373 -37.09864986  14.12532654  -0.65069705  20.57327935  -7.20274424 
##          133          134          135          136          137          138 
##  -0.97876784   1.24520857   6.46918497  -3.30683862  -0.08286222  17.14111418 
##          139          140          141          142          143          144 
##   4.36509059 -16.41093301  16.81304340   6.03701980 -40.73900379 -19.51502739 
##          145          146          147          148          149          150 
## -37.29105098  18.93292542 -32.84309817  -5.61912177 -11.39514536   8.82883104 
##          151          152          153          154          155          156 
##   6.05280744 -20.72321615  34.50076025  16.72473666   0.94871306  28.17268947 
##          157          158          159          160          161          162 
## -17.60333413  14.62064228  34.84461868  19.06859509 -23.70742851  -4.48345210 
##          163          164          165          166          167          168 
##   7.74052430  11.96450071  -2.81152289  -1.58754649  -2.36357008   6.86040632 
##          169          170 
## -39.91561727  -4.69164087
monona_resids <- monona %>% 
  mutate(residuals = resid(lake_mod)) %>% 
  select(year1, duration, residuals)
  
monona_resids
## # A tibble: 170 × 3
##    year1 duration residuals
##    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1  1855      118    -3.54 
##  2  1856      151    29.7  
##  3  1857      119    -2.10 
##  4  1858       94   -26.9  
##  5  1859      111    -9.65 
##  6  1860      129     8.58 
##  7  1861      133    12.8  
##  8  1862      119    -0.976
##  9  1863      131    11.2  
## 10  1864      117    -2.53 
## # ℹ 160 more rows
monona_resids %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year1, y = residuals)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

Linearity

# This is just a helpful function so I don't have to type this out every time;
# feel free to use it yourself or not as you like

resid_plot <- function(data) {
  # assumes the columns are named "x" and "y" respectively
  data %>%
    mutate(resid = resid(lm(y~x, data))) %>% 
    ggplot(aes(x, resid)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0)
}

There should be no obvious curved patterns in a residual plot.

set.seed(2024543)
linear_data <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = 0.05 * (x - 5) + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5)
)

nonlinear_data <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = 0.05 * (x - 5)^2 + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5)
)
resid_plot(nonlinear_data) +
  ggtitle("Linearity Violated: Curved Pattern Present")

resid_plot(linear_data) +
  ggtitle("Linearity Satisfied: No Curved Pattern Present")

Normality

The points in a residual plot should be evenly scattered around the center horizontal line at 0.

set.seed(20240407)
nonnormal_data <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = 0.05 * (x - 5) + sample(c(-2, 2), 100, replace = TRUE) * rnorm(100, mean = 1, sd = 0.5)
)

set.seed(20240407)
nonnormal_data2 <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = 2*x + rgamma(100, 1) - 1
)
resid_plot(nonnormal_data) +
  ggtitle("Normality Violated: Points Don't Tend Towards Center")

resid_plot(nonnormal_data2) +
  ggtitle("Normality Around 0 Violated: Non-Symmetric")

resid_plot(linear_data) +
  ggtitle("Normality Satisfied: Residuals Symmetric Around 0")

Constant variance

The residual plot should show no relationship between x and the spread of residuals. We don’t want to see a “megaphone” shape.

set.seed(20240407)
heteroskedastic_data <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = x + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5 * x)
)

heteroskedastic_data2 <- tibble(
  x = runif(100, 0, 10),
  y = x + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5 * (10-x))
)
resid_plot(heteroskedastic_data) +
  ggtitle("Constant Variance Violated: Spread Increases with X")

resid_plot(heteroskedastic_data2) +
  ggtitle("Constant Variance Violated: Spread Decreases with X")

resid_plot(linear_data) +
  ggtitle("Constant Variance Satisfied: Equal Spread Across X")

Monona data

The lake monona model appears to be well fit.

monona_resids %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year1, y = residuals)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0)

Prediction

How tall was Riley at age 78 months?

ggplot(riley_2_8, aes(x = age, y = height)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "lm") +
  geom_point(aes(x = new_x, y = new_y), color="red", size=4) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 78, color="red", linetype="dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 49.78977, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  ylab("Height (inches)") +
  xlab("Age (months)")
## Error in `geom_point()`:
## ! Problem while computing aesthetics.
## ℹ Error occurred in the 3rd layer.
## Caused by error:
## ! object 'new_x' not found
30.25 + (0.25 * 78)
## [1] 49.75
# or

predict(height_mod, newdata = tibble(age = 78))
##        1 
## 49.78977

Monona data

According to the linear model, what will be the duration of a future freeze? Set year1 to be a number 2026 or greater.

lake_mod <- lm(duration ~ year1, data = monona)

We have to be mindful when doing prediction. Extrapolation can be dangerous - for example, how long will the freeze duration in year 2400 be?
